lever.lever: 12-month postpartum Medicaid extension (46 states + DC by Mar 2024)
lever.movement_note: The bars pair 2022 against 2023. White, Hispanic, and Asian rates all fell, but the Black rate held near 50 per 100k — roughly 3.5x the White rate — and in fact edged up (49.5 to 50.3), so the average moved while the gap did not. The overall rate fell to 18.6 in 2023 (669 deaths vs 817 in 2022); that improvement was statistically significant for White and Hispanic women but not for Black women. These are NCHS counts built on small annual numbers, so single-year moves are noisy — the durable finding is the persistence of the Black-White gap, not any one year's tick. Timing matters: the 12-month postpartum Medicaid extension mostly postdates this 2023 window, so it is the forward lever aimed at the unmoved gap, not the cause of the average's improvement. Decision: target the Black maternal gap specifically — the resources follow the gap that did not move, not the average that did.

The bars pair 2022 (the pandemic trough that triggered tutoring mandates) against 2024. Nationally, grade-8 math did not recover: White held at 284, Black slipped 252 to 251, Hispanic fell 261 to 257 — widening the White-Hispanic gap — while Asian/Pacific Islander was essentially flat (304 to 305). NAEP scores carry sampling error, so single-year moves of a point or two are not by themselves statistically meaningful; the reliable signal is the non-recovery at grade 8. Where recovery did show up it was concentrated: grade 4, and states that mandated high-dosage tutoring — Louisiana was the only state to top its pre-pandemic grade-4 reading, and Alabama posted the nation's largest grade-4 math gain (230 to 236). (Louisiana's grade-4 math also edged above 2019, but not significantly — the durable win was reading.) Decision: the lever works where it is actually deployed at dosage, so the read points funding toward mandated grade-8 tutoring in the states and grades that have not yet scaled it, aimed at the widening Hispanic gap.

lever.lever: OTC naloxone (Narcan approved Mar 2023) + pharmacy dispensing scale-up
lever.movement_note: Age-adjusted overdose death rates fell sharply: total deaths dropped 26.2% to 79,384 in 2024 — the largest one-year decline on record — and every racial group fell. But naloxone was deployed alongside large supply-side shifts (fentanyl saturation, precursor changes) that can dwarf harm-reduction levers in top-line mortality, so the data's role here is evaluative — it detected the turn — more than proof the lever caused it. Read the disparity, not just the drop: the American Indian/Alaska Native rate stays the highest and is almost certainly understated, because racial misclassification on death certificates chronically undercounts AI/AN deaths (and, to a smaller degree, other non-White deaths). Decision: the group with the worst rate is also the least accurately counted, so naloxone saturation and post-overdose outreach should be targeted to AI/AN communities while the death-certificate race coding that hides the true burden gets fixed.

lever.lever: College Scorecard publishes median earnings by specific major at specific school (field-of-study API)
lever.movement_note: Pulled from the College Scorecard field-of-study API — Computer Science (CIP 1107) bachelor's, median earnings four years after completion, Title IV federal-aid recipients only. The striking thing is the spread across schools for the same credential in the same field: Carnegie Mellon 268,121, MIT 225,141, Stanford 214,907, Harvard 203,169 — but CUNY York 73,044 and Fayetteville State 36,164. A CS bachelor's is not one number; where you earn it moves the median five- to sevenfold, and horizon compounds it (Harvard reads 152,251 at one year, 203,169 at four). Two honesty flags. Small cells: at the regional schools the Title-IV completer counts are thin enough that horizons run non-monotonic — Fayetteville reads 50,940 at one year but 36,164 at four — a sign the medians are noisy, not precise. Surface dependence: the same federal source answers differently depending on which door you use — the field-of-study API returns no CS bachelor's figure for Brown at any horizon (its single-year Title-IV completer cohort is below the privacy threshold), yet the public Field of Study web tool shows ~214,000 by pooling two award years into one cohort large enough to clear it. Neither number is wrong; the grain of the query decides whether a number exists at all. Decision: the earnings signal is real enough to steer program-level accountability and student guidance — but only if the grain travels with it, because school x CIP x horizon x aid-subgroup is the unit, and a single headline ('CS pays 250k') strips exactly the frame that makes it true. Whether publishing earnings actually changes behavior is separately unsettled: Hurwitz & Smith found a small, advantage-concentrated shift in where students sent scores; later replications found little sorting toward higher-earning schools.

lever.lever: Virginia's Longitudinal Data System links records across five state agencies (K-12, higher ed, employment, community colleges, IT) to measure post-completion wages
lever.movement_note: Virginia's SCHEV report links K-12, higher-ed, and employment records to publish post-completion wages — median annual wage five years out for 2017-18 graduates, measured on 2023 Unemployment-Insurance records (nominal). At the credential median the premium is smaller than the folk model assumes: a four-year bachelor's runs 65,249, an applied-science (AAS) associate 58,393, and a transfer associate 49,444 — so 'higher credential = higher pay' holds on average but only barely, and the distributions overlap heavily. That overlap is the real finding: at these medians a strong applied-science associate can out-earn a weaker bachelor's, so credential tier alone is a poor predictor of individual pay. Two caveats travel with every wage here: it is UI-covered, in-state employment only (out-of-state, federal, military, self-employed, and gig work are invisible), and not every graduate has a reported wage — match rates differ by credential, so the medians describe differently-selected subpopulations. SCHEV itself warns against ranking programs on these wages without adjusting for family wealth, residency, and demographics. Decision: fund and advise by field-and-region outcome, not by credential tier — and open the record-level data (today locked behind data-sharing agreements) so families and funders can interrogate the aggregate instead of trusting it. Built to decide; not built to browse.
